entry.title: The Patient SAP Technical Architect
entry.content:
I have spent years writing things no human will ever read. Behavior definitions. CDS annotations. Authorization objects with names like F_BKPF_BUK. My work lived in layers below the surface of the enterprise — invisible, load-bearing, essential. The user clicked a button. I was the reason it worked. We never met.

SAP Joule is the first thing in thirty years of SAP's history that changes that relationship. Not for the user — they always had the button. It changes it for me, the architect. Joule is, in some sense, a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote.

I. What It Actually Is

Let us be precise before we are poetic.

Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot, available since late 2023 across S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Analytics Cloud, and BTP. It sits as a panel on the right side of your Fiori Launchpad. You type a question in plain language. It answers using your live SAP data — not a simulation, not a demonstration dataset, not a hallucination dressed in corporate clothing.

The architecture underneath is worth understanding. There are large language models — partnerships with Mistral, Anthropic, and others — running inside SAP AI Core on BTP. But the models alone are not what makes Joule interesting. What makes it interesting is the grounding layer: a proprietary business context engine that tethers every LLM response to real SAP master data, transactional records, and configuration. The authorization layer then filters that response through your role's existing permission objects.

Joule will not invent a vendor name. It will not fabricate an invoice amount. It reads what is actually in your system, through the authorizations you actually hold. This is the thing that separates it from every consumer AI product your CFO has already tried to ban.

Retrieval Augmented Generation fetches SAP Help documentation and live business data at query time. The result is a response grounded in both SAP's knowledge and your company's reality simultaneously. For an architect who has spent years building that reality — one CDS view at a time — this is not a small thing.

II. What Changes for the Developer

In Business Application Studio, Joule is embedded as an intelligent coding assistant that understands not just syntax, but intent within SAP's specific frameworks. This is the distinction that matters.

A general coding assistant knows JavaScript. Joule knows CAP. It knows that a @readonly annotation in a projection entity has specific implications for the generated OData service. It knows RAP — managed and unmanaged scenarios, the difference between a BDEF with managed implementation and one with a handler class. It knows Fiori annotations well enough to suggest @ui.lineItem positioning without being told the rendering context.

Consider what used to take an afternoon:

-- A developer once spent 3-4 hours writing:
-- Entity definition, associations, access controls,
-- projection view, service definition, BDEF,
-- behavior implementation stubs.

-- Now the prompt is:
"Generate a managed RAP Business Object for Equipment
Maintenance. Header has Equipment, Description, PlantCode,
Category. Items have Task, PlannedDate, Status. Include
mandatory field validations and a confirmMaintenance action."

-- Joule produces all of it. In seconds.
Time savings documented in SAP's own benchmarks:
CDS view generation 2–4 hours → 15 minutes.
RAP BDEF generation 3–5 hours → 30 minutes.
Unit test generation 2–3 hours → 20 minutes.

The productivity numbers are real. But productivity is the least interesting thing happening here.

What is actually happening is a shift in what the developer's day consists of. If Joule generates the scaffolding, I am no longer spending my cognitive energy on the ceremony of SAP development — the syntax rituals, the framework boilerplate, the documentation archaeology. I am spending it on the decisions that actually require judgment: data modeling choices, authorization concept design, integration topology, performance trade-offs that the LLM cannot make without business context only I possess.

The parts of the job that were tedious are becoming automated. The parts that required taste are becoming more visible.

III. What Changes for the Architect

The technical architect's role has always been translation. Business says: we need to track equipment maintenance across three plants with approval workflows. The architect translates that into a set of data structures, authorization concepts, integration points, and deployment decisions. Then the architect communicates that translation downward to developers and upward to stakeholders.

Joule compresses the translation loop.

An architect can now ask Joule to explain the current purchasing organization structure of a client system in plain language — not to learn it themselves, but to generate documentation that previously would have taken a day to write. An architect can ask Joule to review a CDS view for performance issues and receive back a structured analysis with specific optimization suggestions. An architect can use Joule in Joule Studio to build custom AI skills that extend Joule's capabilities to the client's specific domain — fleet management, specialized procurement categories, industry-specific compliance — without writing LLM infrastructure from scratch.

The architect who understands Joule's grounding architecture — RAG over SAP content, live data APIs, role-based authorization filtering — can design systems that work with the AI layer rather than around it. This is the new literacy.

Joule Studio is worth dwelling on. It is SAP's low-code environment for building custom AI skills — defining intents, entities, actions, and knowledge bases that extend Joule to domains SAP itself has not anticipated. An architect who can define a skill scope, enumerate the 15 most common user intents, connect them to CAP service endpoints, and upload a policy knowledge base for RAG-based answering is delivering something qualitatively different from the architects who cannot.

The custom skill architecture is clean in its separation of concerns: user request → intent recognition → skill routing → backend API call → structured result → natural language response. Every piece of this maps onto patterns architects already know. The new piece is the intent layer. Learning to design for intent rather than for transaction is the shift.

IV. On Security and the Things Clients Ask

Every architect deploying Joule will face the same questions in the same order. They deserve direct answers.

Does our data go to OpenAI? No. Joule runs LLMs inside SAP AI Core — SAP's tenanted infrastructure on BTP. Your data does not reach public AI providers. The BTP data center region you select determines data residency.

Can users see what they shouldn't? No. Joule inherits your existing SAP authorization objects. The same XSUAA integration and IAM role collections that govern Fiori app access govern what Joule can retrieve and display. An AP clerk asking Joule about payroll data receives the same result as that clerk opening the payroll app directly: nothing.

Does Joule learn from our data and share it? No. Tenant isolation is strict. Your interaction data does not train shared models.

Can Joule take destructive actions? Only within the user's authorization scope, and with explicit confirmation for significant actions. Joule does not bypass workflow approvals. It can initiate them.

The governance framework an architect should recommend to clients has five components: an AI usage policy defining acceptable prompts, a data classification guide for what belongs in prompt text, audit logging via SAP Audit Log Service, access control via BTP Role Collections, and a custom skill review process before any new skills reach production.

This is not exotic. It is the same governance discipline good architects have always applied to system access. The vocabulary is new. The principles are familiar.

V. The Prompting Problem

There is a skill that SAP calls the CRAFT framework — Context, Role, Action, Format, Target — for constructing effective prompts. Architects deploying Joule will find themselves teaching this to end users. They will also need to build what the guide calls a Prompt Library: a collection of validated, role-specific prompts that become a deliverable of the implementation project itself.

This is not trivial. A prompt is a specification. A bad specification produces bad output. The work of prompt engineering is, at its core, the same work as requirements engineering: making implicit intent explicit enough that a system can act on it reliably.

-- Vague (produces vague output):
"Show me sales data"

-- Precise (produces precise output):
"As the Regional Sales Manager, summarize in a table
the top 10 customers by overdue receivables for
company code 1000 as of today, showing amount,
days overdue, and responsible account manager."

Architects who understand why good prompts work — because they reduce the search space of possible interpretations — will build better prompt libraries. They will also build better training programs, because they can explain the underlying principle rather than just distributing a list of magic phrases.

VI. What the Roadmap Actually Means

SAP has stated its intention to make Joule the primary interface for all SAP applications by 2026. The roadmap includes agentic AI — Joule executing multi-step business processes autonomously — and cross-application orchestration: one prompt triggering actions in S/4HANA, Ariba, and SAC simultaneously without human mediation at each step.

Architects should read this carefully.

Agentic AI changes the error model. When a human executes a sequence of SAP transactions, each step is a checkpoint. When an agent executes them, the checkpoints are only where you designed them. The architect who builds agentic workflows must think carefully about where confirmation steps live, what constitutes a reversible versus irreversible action, and how audit trails are maintained across a sequence initiated by a single natural language instruction.

This is not a reason to be afraid of agentic AI. It is a reason for architects to be involved in its design rather than observing from outside. The governance questions — what can an agent do without human confirmation, what triggers a human-in-the-loop, how do we reconcile agent actions with existing workflow approval hierarchies — are architectural questions. They require people who understand both the business process and the technical substrate.

The architects who will matter most in the next five years are not the ones who resist AI augmentation. They are the ones who understand it precisely enough to design its guardrails.

VII. A Note on What Disappears

When a senior user of SAP needs to find the payment status of a vendor invoice, they navigate to FBL1N, enter the vendor number, set the selection criteria, execute the report, read the results. This takes two minutes if you know what you are doing. It takes fifteen minutes if you do not, and it takes a phone call to IT if you have forgotten the T-code entirely.

Joule collapses this to: "What is the payment status for vendor Bosch?"

The T-code does not disappear. The CDS view behind the Fiori app that FBL1N has become does not disappear. The authorization object F_LFA1_BUK that controls whether this user can see this vendor's data does not disappear. Everything I built is still there. What disappears is the requirement that the user know any of it.

This is the correct outcome. Users should never have needed to know T-codes. That was always an implementation detail leaking into the user experience — a failure of interface design that we normalized because we had no better option. Joule is the better option, thirty years late.

For the developer, this means the contract changes. I am no longer primarily responsible for navigation design or information architecture in the traditional sense. I am responsible for the quality of the data models and service definitions that Joule reads. If my CDS associations are poorly structured, Joule's answers will be poorly structured. If my authorization concepts are coarse-grained, Joule's data filtering will be coarse-grained. The user never sees my code. They see Joule's response to my code. The distance between my work and its consequence has compressed.

I began by saying that Joule is a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote. That is not quite right. More precisely: Joule is the interface through which the things I built finally learned to speak for themselves.

The patient labor of enterprise software architecture — the years of CDS views and RAP objects and authorization concepts — was always in service of business processes that required this complexity to function. The complexity was never the goal. The goal was always the vendor getting paid, the employee going on leave, the purchase order reaching the right supplier on time.

Joule does not make the complexity disappear. It makes the complexity invisible, which is what the complexity always wanted to be. My job, as architect, is to ensure the invisible infrastructure is worthy of the interface that now speaks for it.

That is not a smaller job. It is, finally, the right one.

entry.title: Get Early Insights to SAP AI Innovations: Join SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 by June 19
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire, SAP reaffirmed its strong commitment to AI-driven innovation and its role in transforming businesses.

Innovation at SAP is built on collaboration. Leveraging cutting-edge technologies like Artificial Intelligence, SAP continues to drive meaningful business value for customers. Now, we invite you to be part of this journey by participating in SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects focused on AI.

🗓Registration is open until June 19, 2026.

In today’s fast-evolving business landscape, early involvement is key. Through the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative, you can collaborate directly with SAP product managers and developers, sharing feedback on concepts, prototypes, and innovations while they are still taking shape.

View the full list here, or browse the links below to discover the selected exciting opportunities:

SAP Business Technology Platform

  • AI-Assisted Error Remediation for SAP Application Interface Framework
  • AI assisted guidance in the UI theme designer
  • BTP Specific Data Privacy & AI based Data Masking
  • Fulfillment of business needs through Business Adaption Plans and Solution Implementation
  • Partner Perspectives in SAP BTP and AI development

Cloud ERP

  • Accelerate Integrations with AI powered SAP Integration Suite
  • Agentic AI in SAP S/4HANA Procurement (Private Cloud Edition)
  • Agentic AI Smart Billing Assistant
  • AI in SAP GRC for SAP HANA
  • AI-Powered Warranty Verification – Co-Innovate the future of service, repair & claims
  • Intelligent Returns: Process Discovery & Automation Roadmap
  • Modernization Approaches in Purchase Order (SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition)

SAP Globalization

  • AI for UI: Exploring the Usage of AI in Translating SAP's Product User Interface
  • Assessing Demand for AI Speech Services in SAP Translation Hub

SAP Concur

  • Concur Invoice - AI-first, Reimagine Invoice Experience

SAP Customer Engagement Initiative meets SAP Integration Experience Feedback

  • Hands-on: AI-powered Dispute Mgmt Scenario with SAP Enterprise Service Mgmt - Provisioning & Setup
  • Hands-on Experience of AI-powered Dispute Mgmt Scenario with SAP Enterprise Service Management
  • Hands-on System Experience of the AI-Assisted Process Modeler in SAP Signavio

Others

  • Autonomous Supply Chain: Embodied AI in the Warehouse
  • Embodied AI Autonomous Asset Inspection
  • Embodied AI: Autonomous Visual Inspection for Quality and Safety
  • Iterate together on the User Experience for Joule Work
  • Providing feedback on the Discovery domain in SAP LeanIX

Let’s shape the future together!

Innovation happens through collaboration. By participating in the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects, you help SAP create better, more relevant solutions for businesses like yours. This is your chance to influence what comes next.

Don’t miss out – sign up today at influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2026!

Warm regards, 
Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team 

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: Unlock Better Hiring with Qualifications: Start Smart, Scale Faster with SAP Fieldglass
entry.content:
Why Qualifications Matter

Qualifications bring structure and consistency to hiring by replacing free-text job requirements with clearly defined skills. When used consistently, they improve candidate matching, enable better system recommendations, and create a reusable framework that scales with your organization. Without this structure, job postings vary widely and AI features lack the context needed to be effective.

The Hidden Cost of Skipping Day 1 Setup

Features like Recommend Qualifications depend entirely on an existing qualification library. Without it, the system has no reference point, and recommendations cannot work. Creating this library manually can feel time-consuming, which is why many teams delay it—and often never return to it.

The Solution: Generate Qualifications from Existing Data

Instead of starting from scratch, you can use Generate Qualifications to build your library instantly using AI. The system analyzes your existing job postings and templates, extracts commonly used skills, and creates a recommended list of qualifications. In one step, you move from having no structure to a usable, standardized framework.

What You Need to Do (Day One Setup)

  1. Upload your job postings or templates
  2. Contact your Configuration Manager to run Generate Qualifications
  3. Review and select the relevant qualifications

This creates a ready-to-use qualification library that supports both job postings and recommendations.

Activating Recommend Qualifications

Once your library is in place, enable the Recommend Qualifications feature. During job posting creation, the system suggests relevant qualifications based on your job description—using your own structured data.

Key insight: The quality of recommendations depends on the quality of your library.

How This Improves Hiring

With qualifications in place, recruiters can create consistent job postings, rely on better recommendations, and improve candidate matching. This leads to faster, more accurate hiring decisions without adding complexity.

Final Thoughts

Qualifications are a foundational step in skill-based hiring. By setting them up early using Generate Qualifications, you can quickly unlock the value of AI features and create a more consistent, scalable hiring process.

Start with the data you already have, build your qualification library, and let the system do the rest.

You can also have a look at the following topic in the What’s New Viewer for SAP Fieldglass for more details: 

AI Assisted Qualification Generation for Qualification Library

entry.title: CX Onboarding - New Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Playlist on AI
entry.content:
New Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Playlist on AI: How AI Is Transforming Customer Experience

The business world is changing fast, and customer expectations are higher than ever. Today, customers expect seamless, personalized experiences at every touchpoint—and artificial intelligence is no longer a nice-to-have, but a fundamental capability to deliver them.

AI is transforming how organizations operate by enabling them to better understand customer behavior, anticipate needs, and respond with precision. What was once reactive and manual is becoming proactive and intelligent, allowing businesses to move faster, make better decisions, and create more meaningful connections with their customers.

At SAP, this transformation goes beyond isolated AI use cases. It is part of a broader vision: the Autonomous Enterprise. In this model, end-to-end business processes are powered by AI and organized across core functions such as Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, Human Capital Management, and Customer Experience. AI agents operate across these domains, executing complex workflows autonomously while maintaining enterprise governance and control.

Within this vision, Customer Experience plays a central role. Autonomous CX brings together CRM and customer experience processes into a unified system, where AI acts on a complete, real-time view of your business. This enables organizations to deliver consistent, intelligent, and highly personalized experiences at scale.

This transformation becomes tangible in solutions like SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2, where AI is embedded directly into everyday workflows.

In customer service, AI helps teams move from information overload to instant expertise. Capabilities such as case management assistance streamline case preparation, processing, and resolution while learning from every interaction—turning expert knowledge into reusable insights for the entire team.

In sales, AI enables a shift from data overload to guided selling. Intelligent assistants help prioritize opportunities, identify risks early, and automate time-consuming tasks like quote generation, allowing sales teams to focus on what matters most: closing deals and building relationships.

Together, these capabilities represent a fundamental shift in how Customer Experience is delivered in both efficiency and better outcomes for customers.

As AI adoption continues to accelerate, many organizations are looking for practical ways to get started and realize value quickly—especially during onboarding and implementation phases.

To support our customers on this journey, the CX Onboarding team has created a 📺comprehensive video playlist for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2. This playlist is designed to introduce the key AI capabilities available within the solutions and help you accelerate adoption, maximize ROI, and build a strong foundation for success.

👇Read below to learn more.

Introduction to SAP Business AI

Learn why AI is more important than ever and about SAP’s integrated approach to Customer Experience and intelligent applications. It shows how SAP connects CX with backend systems through a unified data layer, enabling real-time insights and seamless collaboration across business functions.

Joule, SAP’s Business Copilot

Get introduced to Joule, SAP’s AI copilot, and how it enables natural, conversational interaction with business systems. Joule helps users access information quickly, automate tasks, and navigate to the right applications, all while integrating with tools like Microsoft 365.

AI Agents for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

This video explains what AI agents are and how they operate autonomously to perform tasks with minimal human intervention. It showcases use cases such as automatic case classification and a Q&A agent that improves response accuracy and consistency.

Generative AI Features for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

This session focuses on generative AI and how it creates new content based on learned patterns. It demonstrates features such as Lead Booster, which enhances customer understanding, and the Email Draft Recommender, which helps streamline communication.

Machine Learning Features for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2

Explore how machine learning helps systems learn from data and improve over time. Become familiar with its capabilities such as Lead Intelligence, which predicts conversion likelihood, and Case Topic Analyzer, which organizes case data into meaningful insights.

🔍Explore our playlist to learn how to make the most out of AI with Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you for reading this blog. We hope you found the content insightful. Please give us a like or leave a comment; we’d love to hear from you!

You can request a one‑on‑one call with one of our onboarding advisors using this form. 

💻Want to get in touch with us? Send an email to SAP_CX_Onboarding@sap.com.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

👉Key Resources

  • Onboarding Resource Center: SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2
  • AI guide for Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2
  • Joule Agents and Joule Assistants for customer experience
  • SAP Business AI in the Discovery Center

entry.title: Building a Native MCP Server in SAP ABAP: Enabling AI Agents to Talk to Your SAP System
entry.content:
Hi All!

The content of this blog was initially written in Chinese by myself (Not AI), but later it was translated into English with the help of AI, and the AI also enhanced and structured the content. And the content reflects my own experiences and thoughts, and includes both text and images.


I'm a bit of a newcomer when it comes to posting here — but definitely not a stranger to here. I've been lurking and learning from your blogs for over a decade, and countless threads here have saved me in real-world projects. So thank you all!

Recently, I built something that genuinely excited me, and I wanted to share it with this community. This post is about how I explored AI Agents, connected them with SAP, and ultimately built a fully custom MCP Server natively inside SAP ABAP — enabling AI Agents to interact directly with SAP business processes using natural language.

 

Background: The Mission

Back in early January this year, my team lead gave me an open-ended challenge: while continuing to support our current work, explore how AI can drive business innovation.

I dove in. Here's what I explored:

  • Set up a local AI model (Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) on my Windows PC refering the ModelScope.
  • Built a simple Python-based Chat Agent with VS Code extensions (Lingma/Copilot)
  • Created a local MCP Server (Python-based using Lingma/Copilot) with tools for things like: checking GPU status, logging into SAP, downloading email attachments from Outlook, detecting invoice files and auto-sorting them

The result: I could have natural language conversations with the AI, and it would intelligently call these tools to get things done. Pretty cool!

 

The First Breakthrough — and the Problem It Revealed

After building the local MCP Server, I started thinking: what if I could make the AI Agent interact directly with SAP?

I added a tool to my local MCP Server that called an SAP API. It worked! The AI Agent could now talk to SAP. I was thrilled.

But then reality hit.

Every single SAP API endpoint required:

  1. Creating a new API endpoint on the SAP side
  2. Adding a new tool in the Python MCP Server to call that endpoint
  3. Manually handling and exposing all the input parameters

Scale that to hundreds or thousands of business scenarios, and the maintenance burden would be enormous. Worse, the tight coupling between the Python MCP Server and the SAP system felt architecturally wrong. I wanted something more elegant — a decoupled, generalized framework.

So the question became: What if I built the MCP Server directly inside SAP?

 

The Solution: A Native SAP ABAP MCP Server

Learning MCP First

Before building anything, I went back to basics. I studied the Model Context Protocol (MCP) from the official documentation. And combined with video tutorials, I got a clear picture of how MCP works: it's a standard protocol that defines how AI Agents discover and call tools, enabling LLMs to interact with external systems in a structured way.

System Architecture

Here's the high-level architecture I designed:

All MCP interactions — initialization, tool listing, and tool invocation — go through a single SAP ICF endpoint. The SAP system itself acts as the MCP Server.

 

Key ABAP Components

Here is a breakdown of the core classes and their roles:

The ICF HTTP Handler Class

Registered as an ICF service in SICF, this class is the entry point. It receives all incoming HTTP requests from the AI Agent and delegates them to the MCP Server.

The Core MCP Server Class

This is the brain. It handles:

  • Tool registration (In CONSTRUCTOR via the Tool Registry Class)
  • Request parsing 
  • MCP Initialization handshake 
  • Tool listing 
  • Tool invocation
  • JSON response building for the AI Agent

The Tool Registry Class

A central registry where all MCP tools are registered. When the MCP Server initializes, it queries this registry to build the tool catalog that gets exposed to AI Agents.

The Tool Interface

A standard ABAP interface that every tool class must implement. It defines:

  • Tool metadata: name, description (sent to the LLM so it knows when to use this tool)
  • Input schema: parameter definitions (name, type, description, required flag)
  • Execution logic: how to process the AI Agent's request and return a result

A Demo Tool Class

A sample tool that queries customer data. It demonstrates the full pattern:

  1. Define tool name and description
  2. Define input schema 
  3. Receive input from the AI Agent, parse it into ABAP fields
  4. Execute ABAP business logic
  5. Serialize result as JSON and return it up the chain: MCP Server → HTTP Handler → Agent → LLM → User

Key Design Principle: One Endpoint to Rule Them All

The most important design decision here is that the entire MCP protocol is served through a single ICF endpoint. Whether the AI Agent is initializing, requesting the tool list, or calling a specific tool — it all goes to the same URL. The MCP Server internally routes based on the method name in the request payload.

This dramatically simplifies the SAP-side setup. Adding a new business tool means:

  1. Create a new ABAP class implementing the tool interface.
  2. Register it in configuration table.
  3. Done — no new ICF services, no new HTTP configurations.

💡The LLM automatically discovers all registered tools through the tool listing handshake. You define the tool's description carefully, and the model figures out when to call it.

Funny work: You can try to use AI coding tools (Lingma/Copilot) to help write ABAP classes based on the Key ABAP Components part.

 

Results & Demo

By late January / early February, I had a fully functional, self-built SAP MCP Server running in our SAP system. I demoed it to the team, and in the following two months I refined it further, while also exploring broader Agent application topics like RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), Skills, and enterprise-grade Agent frameworks.

For demo screenshots showing real AI Agent ↔ SAP interactions, please refer the belows:

 

AI Agent Applications Used for Testing

I used existing AI Agent products as the client-side interface — no custom frontend needed:

  • GitHub Copilot CLI
  • Dify Platform
  • Codebuddy CLI
  • AnythingLLM

All of these support the MCP protocol, so they connect to the SAP MCP Server out of the box. Download and install any of them to try this yourself.

 

 

What's Next?

This is just the beginning. Some areas I'm actively exploring:

  • Expanding the tool library with more business-relevant SAP tools
  • Connecting multiple SAP systems through a federated MCP Server pattern
  • Integrating RAG to give the AI Agent access to SAP documentation and business knowledge

 

Closing Thoughts

I built this because I wanted to prove that SAP and modern AI Agents can work together natively — without heavy middleware or expensive third-party platforms. The MCP standard makes this surprisingly achievable even from a pure ABAP background.

If you're an ABAPer curious about AI, or an AI enthusiast wondering how to connect LLMs to SAP — I hope this inspires you to experiment. The barrier is lower than you think.

Would love to hear your thoughts, questions, or ideas in the comments. Let's explore this together! 🚀

entry.title: Start Small. Think at Scale. Business Professionals Can Use SAP & Claude to Build a Business Case.
entry.content:
Note: This blog provides an actionable step-by-step proof of concept (POC) for the SAP and Anthropic partnership that a business professional can build today using SAP's free Business Accelerator Hub sandbox data and Claude. The Master Prompt is included below. A sample output is provided in the Addendum. Already familiar with this context? Skip directly to The Proof of Concept section.

Build the POC. Create the Business Case. Start the Conversation.

The recent SAP and Anthropic partnership announcement ignited a new wave of opportunities. It got me thinking about how the combined capabilities of Claude and SAP will strengthen enterprises around the globe.


At the same time, I found myself wondering. How can Business Professionals add real value to what needs to happen to put these capabilities in place for maximum success?

It reminded me of a simple metaphor. Imagine digging a new swimming pool with a shovel. You are making progress. Slowly. Painfully. Then someone pulls up with a bulldozer. The hole that would have taken weeks gets done in a day. That is the uplift the SAP and Anthropic partnership represents for organizations who are ready to act.

So how can Business Professionals help jumpstart the process?

Consider the impact of creating a simple proof of concept using SAP's own sandbox data and Claude, grounded in business case best practices, to help prioritize initiatives that can create compelling value.

The Market Opportunity

The scale of this partnership is significant. SAP and Anthropic are not simply connecting two products. They are building an Autonomous Enterprise vision where Claude powered agents operate through SAP Business AI Platform, coordinate across SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Ariba, and other SAP products via Model Context Protocol (MCP), and are accessible to users through Joule.

IDC projects AI spending driven by agentic AI will reach $1.3 trillion by 2029, growing at 31.9% year over year. In a best case scenario Gartner projects a $450 billion enterprise AI opportunity by 2035. Yet according to the DSAG Investment Report published in February 2026, among SAP customers already using AI, only 3% are running SAP Business AI in production today.

The opportunity is enormous. But before Business Professionals can seize it, they must consider creating POCs and business cases that will move their organization from insight to action.

The New API Policy. A Quality Framework.

It is worth highlighting a recent SAP announcement that is directly relevant to this partnership. SAP's API policy governs how autonomous external agents make sequential API calls into SAP systems.

Consider this approach as a quality framework.

Think Lean Six Sigma, ITIL, or ISO 9001 for mission critical Cloud ERP processes. Controlled variation. Trusted inputs. Governed outputs.

Claude operating inside SAP's approved architecture brings these same quality principles to agentic AI. As a Six Sigma certified practitioner I can say with confidence that controlled variation and governed outputs are not optional in mission critical processes. They are the standard.

Errors in enterprise data cannot be fully eliminated but they must be managed, minimized, and controlled continuously. They stem from uncontrolled inputs, ungoverned processes, and systems operating outside defined boundaries.

SAP's framework controls that variation at the source, reducing risk to the lowest achievable level and keeping mission critical processes operating with the precision enterprises depend on.

From Insight to Action. Building Your Case

Consider this scenario. You are a Business Professional who has just learned about the SAP and Anthropic partnership. You see the opportunity clearly. You know your organization has processes that AI can benefit, especially with Claude and SAP working together.

But where do you start?

You are not a developer. You do not have a project budget approved. You cannot stand up a Joule environment this afternoon.

What you do have is business knowledge, SAP familiarity, and a growing confidence in what Claude can do. Most importantly you have a clear sense of where the pain and inefficiencies are in your organization today.

That is enough to get started.

The path forward has two parallel tracks that reinforce each other:

  1. Delivering a proof of concept (POC) that shows what is possible
  2. Creating a business case that justifies the investment to make it real at scale.

The good news is that SAP has already given you the tools to do both.

The SAP Business Accelerator Hub was built explicitly to accelerate customer time to value. Free to access. No SAP license required. No IT ticket needed. Just a free SAP ID and a clear business question you want Claude to help answer.

The proof of concept you build today becomes the evidence that funds the enterprise deployment tomorrow. The business case you build around it gives your organization's executive sponsors the confidence to act.

This approach is how Business Professionals provide meaningful leadership, collaborating with IT counterparts and key stakeholders to move from insight to action.

The Proof of Concept  

This POC is intentionally simple and uses SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition procurement data available through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox. It is a minimum viable product (MVP) approach designed to show what is possible without requiring engineering support, IT involvement, or a live SAP system. Every step is something a Business Professional can execute independently.

What you are about to build is a working example of agentic AI. Claude is not simply answering a question. It is reasoning over real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition data, identifying risk, prioritizing action, and recommending next steps. That is agentic AI in action. And it is a preview of what runs inside Joule at enterprise scale.

The following steps walk you through the POC from start to finish.

What You Do

  1. Register for a free SAP ID at api.sap.com. Takes a few minutes.
  2. Go to the Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001) in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This is the current OData V4 Purchase Order API for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and one example of the many sandbox APIs available across SAP's core product portfolio.
  3. Click “Try Out” at the top. Select “Sandbox” from the dropdown on the right side. Navigate to the Purchase Order section in the left hand navigation. Click the /PurchaseOrder “GET” option. Click “Run.”
  4. Copy the JSON response that appears under “Response Body.”
  5. Open Claude at claude.ai. Paste the data and enter the Master Prompt below. Note: A free Claude account is sufficient for this POC. Claude Pro provides higher usage limits if needed.

What Claude Does

  1. Reads and interprets the SAP S/4HANA Cloud purchase order data.
  2. Identifies which exceptions represent the highest business risk.
  3. Assigns a risk score to each record with a clear rationale.
  4. Recommends specific actions for each prioritized item.
  5. Produces a scored table ready for your business case conversation.
  6. Offers to draft a supplier communication for the highest risk item.

Master Prompt

Copy and paste the following prompt into Claude along with your JSON data (as noted above). You are reviewing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition purchase order data retrieved directly from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox via the Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001).

[START PROMPT]

Review the records provided and analyze the following fields: PurchaseOrder number, Supplier, CreationDate, PurchaseOrderDate, PurchasingProcessingStatus, PurchaseOrderDeletionCode, ReleaseIsNotCompleted, and PurchasingReleaseStrategy.

Based on your analysis:

  1. Identify purchase orders that require attention. Focus on deletion flagged records, incomplete releases, unusual processing statuses, and supplier concentration patterns.
  2. Assign a priority level of High, Medium, or Low to each flagged record with a brief rationale.
  3. Recommend a specific action for each flagged item.
  4. Produce a prioritized table with the following columns: PO Number, Supplier, Creation Date, Processing Status, Deletion Flag, Release Complete, Priority Level, Recommended Action.
  5. Provide a two to three sentence plain English summary of your overall findings before the table.
  6. After the table offer to draft a supplier communication for the highest priority item.
  7. Keep your total response concise. Summary, table, and offer only. No additional commentary unless asked.

[END PROMPT]

What You Just Built

A working example of agentic AI reasoning over real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition procurement data. Built by a Business Professional. Using SAP's own tools. View the sample output in the Addendum below

This is your building block. The cornerstone of your business case. And the start of a meaningful conversation with your IT counterparts, key stakeholders, and executive sponsors about what is possible and the value this use case can deliver.

Start small. Think at scale.

From POC to Production

This POC uses the SAP Business Accelerator Hub sandbox as a starting point outside of SAP's production architecture. That is intentional. The goal is to demonstrate the concept quickly and accessibly. Free sandbox data. No infrastructure required.

Joule Studio is where developers and business teams can go next. A governed, enterprise grade environment where Business Professionals and developers collaborate to build both pre-built and custom agents tailored to their specific processes, data, and workflows. Joule Studio requires SAP BTP, a Joule subscription, SAP Build licensing, and IT provisioning to activate. It is the right next step. See Activate Joule Studio in 5 Simple Steps for full prerequisites.  

Complementary approaches. Each with its own role.

In production this same capability runs natively inside SAP Business AI Platform through the MCP Gateway. It is accessible to users through Joule. Everything you just demonstrated is the reasoning capability that sits behind the potential production implementation. The logic transfers directly.

In this POC the Hub provides everything you need to show what is possible.

Building the Business Case

A strong business case requires the right elements addressed with clarity and evidence. The POC you just built directly addresses two of the most critical. It defines the problem in concrete terms and demonstrates measurable value, ROI potential, and a clear view of what is possible.

The implementation path and risk and governance elements are addressed by SAP's approved architecture. The path runs through SAP Business AI Platform, Joule, and the production framework SAP and Anthropic have built together.

That represents a credible and governed foundation for any business case conversation.


Security and Data You Can Depend On

The same security and governance framework that protects your SAP environment today protects every action Claude takes inside it. The controls do not change because AI is involved. They extend to cover it.

Every action is governed by the same role-based access controls your organization operates under today. Every decision Claude makes is traceable through a full audit trail.

Connecting external data sources to the SAP environment requires proper integration through the SAP Integration Suite, ensuring data flows through governed, secure pathways before Claude ever touches it.

The Path Forward

The SAP and Anthropic partnership is designed for enterprise scale, grounded in trust and quality outcomes. Today, Business Professionals can create a tangible starting point and play a leadership role in the conversation with IT and business stakeholders about initiatives that can create lasting value.

SAP and Anthropic have announced plans to bring Claude to SAP Business AI Platform. Joule Studio, the development environment where agents are built and deployed, has general availability planned for later this year.

Now is the time to build your POC and business case so your organization is ready.

Take it one step at a time.

Start small. Think at scale.

Join the Conversation

Your likes and comments are welcome and appreciated.

  • Are you excited about the SAP and Anthropic partnership?
  • Were you aware of the SAP Business Accelerator Hub?
  • What are you working on with Claude or your favorite Assistant AI that can be applied to the Autonomous Enterprise vision?

About the Author

Rich Blumberg is an award-winning writer and author of two Amazon published books, Novi’s Journey to the Magic Garden and Job Seeking Warriors – A Mentors Guide to Winning. He's the President of World Sales Solutions, LLC, (WSS) a management consulting, low-code, no-code, and AI services firm. For more than 20 years he has worked as an SAP consultant partnering with SAP and their ecosystem organizations around the globe. Outside of work Rich serves as a Drexel University Alumni Board of Governors Emeritus, an ENGin English tutor supporting Ukrainian citizens, and an aspiring guitar player.

Related Blogs

  • How the AI Content Lifecycle Turns Complexity into Clarity at Scale(Part 2)
  • AI-Powered Knowledge Gap Analysis and Content Lifecycle Governance(Part 1)
  • You Can Build an Agentic AI Workflow. No Coding, No Engineering, No Problem.
  • AI at Warp Speed: Lessons from the Horse and Buggy Collapse

Addendum SAP S/4HANA Cloud POC Output

Purchase Order Analysis using the SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox and Claude

Summary

Of the 50 purchase orders reviewed, 6 require immediate attention. Two records carry both a deletion flag and an incomplete release status, representing the highest risk. Four additional records show a deletion code with a completed processing status, suggesting they were cancelled but may warrant confirmation. Supplier 17300001 dominates the dataset with over 60% concentration, which itself warrants a supplier risk review.

Note: In a production environment this same analysis can run across thousands of purchase orders in seconds.

Prioritized Purchase Order Exception Table

Draft Supplier Communication

Highest Priority Item: Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022 | Supplier USSU-VSF01

Subject: Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022 – Urgent Status Clarification Required

Dear USSU-VSF01 Team,

I am writing regarding two purchase orders currently showing an unresolved status in our SAP S/4HANA system that require your immediate attention.

Purchase Orders 4500000021 and 4500000022, both dated August 18, 2016, are currently flagged for deletion while the approval release process remains incomplete. This combination of statuses prevents us from properly closing or reactivating these orders and creates a compliance risk in our procurement records.

We kindly request that you confirm the following by end of business this week:

  1. Whether any goods or services were delivered against either purchase order.
  2. Whether any outstanding invoices or payment obligations remain.
  3. Your agreement to formally close both orders if no outstanding obligations exist.

Please contact our procurement team at your earliest convenience so we can resolve this matter and ensure our records accurately reflect the current state of our relationship.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this request.

Best regards,
Procurement Team
Company Code 1710

Data source: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Purchase Order API (CE_PURCHASEORDER_0001) via SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox. Analysis performed by

entry.title: Building an LLM Agent using CAP
entry.content:
Agentic systems are all the rage these days, and for good reason. They allow us to build AI systems that can iteratively work towards solutions. And while there are many frameworks and tools available for building standalone agents, the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model provides a unique opportunity to build agents that capture domain knowledge and can easily integrate with SAP systems and services.
In this tutorial, we will build a simple LLM agent using CAP.

Agenda:

  • Overview
  • Initialize the CAP app
  • Connecting to aicore with sap-ai-sdk
  • Langchain: Calling an agent
  • Langchain: Calling a tool
  • Create a data model and a service
  • Calling the agent in CAP with a query tool (read)
  • Calling the agent in CAP with an action tool (write)
  • What's Next

Overview

First, what is an agent? There are many definitions out there, but here are a few that I like:

"Agents combine language models with tools to create systems that can reason about tasks, decide which tools to use, and iteratively work towards solutions." - langchain
"Agents are systems that independently accomplish tasks on your behalf." - openai
"An LLM in a loop with an objective" - Simon Willison
"An LLM, some tools and a loop meet in a bar" - me

That last one makes for some good jokes.

In essence, the agent controls the interaction between the model, the tools, and the user.

The tool output is fed back into the model as context for the next step, allowing the agent to iteratively work towards a solution. The loop is continued until the model outputs a final answer or meets a predefined stopping condition like a maximum iteration count. This is what allows agents to handle complex tasks that require multiple steps and interactions with external systems.

TLDR;

If you just want a quick example, here you go. If you want to understand how to integrate agents into a CAP application step by step, read on.

import cds from '@sap/cds'
import { OrchestrationClient } from '@sap-ai-sdk/langchain'
import { createAgent, tool, SystemMessage, HumanMessage } from 'langchain'
import { z } from 'zod'

// aicore models: https://me.sap.com/notes/3437766
const model = new OrchestrationClient({
  promptTemplating: { model: { name: 'mistralai--mistral-small' } }
})

class TravelService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    const tripSearch = tool(
      async ({ begin, end }) => {
        const { Trips } = this.entities
        const q = SELECT.from(Trips).where `beginDate >= ${begin} and endDate <= ${end}`
        const availableTrips = await this.run(q)
        return JSON.stringify(availableTrips)
      },
      {
        name: 'tripSearch',
        description: 'Find trips by date range',
        schema: z.object({
          begin: z.iso.date().describe('begin date, format YYYY-MM-DD'),
          end: z.iso.date().describe('end date, format YYYY-MM-DD')
        })
      }
    )

    const agent = createAgent({
      model, tools: [tripSearch],
      systemPrompt: 'Be a helpful travel assistant.',
    })

    this.on('invokeAgent', async function(req) {
      const systemMessage = new SystemMessage(`Today is ${new Date().toDateString()}`)
      const userMessage = new HumanMessage(req.data.input)
      const result = await agent.invoke({ messages: [systemMessage, userMessage] })
      return result.messages.at(-1).content
    })

    return super.init()
  }
}

export default TravelService

Initialize the CAP app

To get started, we will initialize a new CAP project using the cds init command. This will create a new directory with the necessary files and folders for our CAP application. For this tutorial, we will use the Node.js runtime.

cds init cap-agent --nodejs
code cap-agent  # open in vscode

Connecting to aicore with sap-ai-sdk

We will use the sap-ai-sdk to connect our CAP application to the SAP AI Core service. This will allow us to call a variety of language models from our CAP application.

First, make sure that you have an SAP AI Core service instance. The setup is described here: https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core/sap-ai-core-service-guide/initial-setup

If using the Cloud Foundry cli, you can create a service instance like this:

cf create-service aicore extended agent-ai

Now we can use cds bind to create a service key and save a reference to it in .cdsrc-private.json. This will come in handy later when we want to try it out.

cds bind -2 agent-ai

Next install the sap-ai-sdk package. There are multiple packages available, we want the langchain compatible one. Also install langchain (for creating agents) and zod (for tool schema validation).

npm install "@sap-ai-sdk/langchain" langchain zod

Finally, we need to set our project to use ES modules in order to use the sap-ai-sdk package and more importantly top level await.

# set type to module in package.json
npm pkg set type=module

For information on CommonJS usage, refer to this blog post.

We can test out our connection by creating a simple test script, let's save it at test/ai-sdk.js:

// test/sap-ai-sdk.js
import { OrchestrationClient } from '@sap-ai-sdk/langchain';
import { HumanMessage, SystemMessage } from 'langchain';

const model = new OrchestrationClient({
  promptTemplating: {
    model: {
      // aicore models: https://me.sap.com/notes/3437766
      // name: 'gpt-5',
      // name: 'anthropic--claude-4.5-haiku',
      name: 'mistralai--mistral-small',        // cheap for testing
    }
  }
});

const systemMessage = new SystemMessage('Be a helpful assistant!');
const userMessage = new HumanMessage('How many p in strawberry?')
const response = await model.invoke([systemMessage, userMessage]);

console.log(response.content)

Now let's utilize our earlier cds bind reference to run this script with the correct environment variables:

cds bind --exec -- node test/ai-sdk.js

Langchain: Calling an agent

If you're familiar with agents, you can skip this section and jump directly to calling an agent from CAP. But if you're new to agents, this section is for you.

As mentioned earlier, agents combine language models with tools. In their simplest form, an agent contains a model and no tools. So the output is similar to calling the model directly.

const agent = createAgent({
  model: model,
  tools: [],
})

The agent.invoke method is slightly different than the model.invoke method. It expects an object with a messages property.

const messages = [systemMessage, userMessage];
const result = await agent.invoke({ messages });

Let's save this code in a new file test/agent.js and run it:

// test/agent.js
import { OrchestrationClient } from '@sap-ai-sdk/langchain';
import { createAgent, HumanMessage, SystemMessage } from 'langchain';

const model = new OrchestrationClient({
  promptTemplating: { model: { name: 'mistralai--mistral-small' } }
});


const agent = createAgent({
  model: model,
  tools: [],
})

const systemMessage = new SystemMessage('Be a helpful assistant!');
const userMessage = new HumanMessage('How many p in strawberry?')
const messages = [systemMessage, userMessage];
const result = await agent.invoke({ messages });

console.log(result.messages.at(-1).content)
cds bind --exec -- node test/agent.js

Now this is a very simple agent that doesn't do much more than the model itself, but it sets the stage for adding tools and iterating in a loop based on tool output, which is where the real power of agents comes in.

Langchain: Calling a tool

A tool in langchain consists of two parts: a function that performs some action, and a schema that tells the agent how to use the tool. There's also a name and description that helps the agent decide when to use the tool.

Let's say we have some data about available trips...

const availableTrips = [{
  beginDate: '2026-06-13',
  endDate: '2026-06-14',
  description: 'Weekend trip to Heidelberg featuring castle sightseeing, hiking and excellent cuisine.'
}, {
  beginDate: '2026-06-13',
  endDate: '2026-06-13',
  description: 'Daytrip to Europa-Park with exciting rollercoasters.'
}]

We can create a tool that searches for trips based on a date range.

const tripSearch = tool(
  ({ begin, end }) => {
    const trips = availableTrips.filter(t => t.beginDate >= begin && t.endDate <= end)
    return JSON.stringify(trips)
  },
  {
    name: 'tripSearch',
    description: 'Find trips by date range',
    schema: z.object({
      begin: z.iso.date().describe('begin date, format YYYY-MM-DD'),
      end: z.iso.date().describe('end date, format YYYY-MM-DD')
    })
  }
)

Focussing on the schema for a moment, we are using zod to define the expected input format for the tool. The agent will use this schema to provide suitable inputs. Furthermore, the agent uses the schema definition to validate the inputs before calling the tool.

In this case, the tool expects an object with begin and end properties, both of which should be date strings in ISO format YYYY-MM-DD.

The validated inputs are then passed to the defined function, which performs the search and returns the results as a JSON string.

Important Note: The validation only checks for the technical correctness of the input. It is still possible for the agent to provide semantically incorrect inputs that pass validation. This can also be maliciously exploited, so the inputs need to be handled as untrusted data. For example, be aware of potential SQL injection if the tool interacts with a database.

Now we can add this tool to our agent and test it out. Let's save this in a new file test/agent-tool.js:

// test/agent-tool.js
import { OrchestrationClient } from '@sap-ai-sdk/langchain';
import { createAgent, HumanMessage, SystemMessage, tool } from 'langchain';
import { z } from 'zod';

const model = new OrchestrationClient({
  promptTemplating: { model: { name: 'mistralai--mistral-small' } }
});


const availableTrips = [{
  beginDate: '2026-06-13',
  endDate: '2026-06-14',
  description: 'Weekend trip to Heidelberg featuring castle sightseeing, hiking and excellent cuisine.'
}, {
  beginDate: '2026-06-13',
  endDate: '2026-06-13',
  description: 'Daytrip to Europa-Park with exciting rollercoasters.'
}]

const tripSearch = tool(
  ({ begin, end }) => {
    const trips = availableTrips.filter(t => t.beginDate >= begin && t.endDate <= end)
    return JSON.stringify(trips)
  },
  {
    name: 'tripSearch',
    description: 'Find trips by date range',
    schema: z.object({
      begin: z.iso.date().describe('begin date, format YYYY-MM-DD'),
      end: z.iso.date().describe('end date, format YYYY-MM-DD')
    })
  }
)


const agent = createAgent({
  model: model,
  tools: [tripSearch],
})

// Our model needs to know what date it is, so we provide it in the system prompt.
// The date is hardcoded that our model picks a deterministic weekend.
const systemMessage = new SystemMessage('Be a helpful assistant! Today is 2026-06-10');
const userMessage = new HumanMessage('Find me a relaxing trip for the next weekend')
const messages = [systemMessage, userMessage];
const result = await agent.invoke({ messages });

console.log('=== Tool calls ===')
console.log(result.messages.slice(2, 4))

console.log('=== Answer ===')
console.log(result.messages.at(-1).content)
cds bind --exec -- node test/agent-tool.js

The agent should recognize that the user is asking for a trip recommendation, decide to use the tripSearch tool and use the answer for the final response.

Notice that we've also adjusted the system prompt to include the "current" date:

// The date is hardcoded that our model picks a deterministic weekend.
const systemMessage = new SystemMessage('Be a helpful assistant! Today is 2026-06-10');

If we do not provide the current date, the model typically falls back to its training data... in my case, July 2024. Not quite ideal for planning a weekend trip in 2026. Feel free to experiment with different dates and see how it affects the agent's behavior. You can also try asking for trips on specific dates or with different criteria to see how the agent utilizes the tool.

Create a data model and a service

Now that we know how to build an agent, let's see how we can integrate it into a CAP application with a real data model and service.

First, let's create a simple data model for storing trip information. We can define this in the db/schema.cds file:

// db/schema.cds
namespace example.travel;
using { User } from '@sap/cds/common';


entity Trips {
  key ID       : Integer @readonly;
  description  : String(2048);
  beginDate    : Date default $now;
  endDate      : Date default $now;
  bookingFee   : Decimal(9,4) default 0;
  currency     : String(3) default 'EUR';
  agency       : Association to Agencies;
  bookings     : Association to many Bookings on bookings.trip = $self;
}

entity Agencies {
    key ID : Integer @readonly;
    name   : String(256);
}


entity Bookings {
  key trip        : Association to Trips;
  key user        : User @readonly @(cds.on.insert: $user);
      bookingDate : Date default $now;
}

Then define a customer facing service in srv/travel-service.cds:

// srv/travel-service.cds
using { example.travel } from '../db/schema';

@rest // or any other protocol you like
service TravelService {
    @readonly entity Trips as projection on travel.Trips
    actions {
        action book() returns String;
    };

    @readonly entity Agencies as projection on travel.Agencies;
    @readonly entity Bookings as projection on travel.Bookings where $user = user;
}

This service exposes the Trips and Agencies entities and also defines a custom action book for booking a trip. We will implement the logic for this action later.

Next, we need some sample data. Always use cds add data to add syntactically correct sample data. Let's add some trips and agencies:

cds add data -n 5 --filter Agencies
cds add data -n 10 --filter Trips

The test data is generated randomly, but an LLM can be used to generate meaningful test data while adhering to the existing schema. For example:

 db/data/example.travel-Trips.csv 

ID,description,beginDate,endDate,bookingFee,currency,agency_ID
2001,"7-Night Maldives Overwater Bungalow Retreat — private chef dinners, unlimited snorkeling excursions, sunset dolphin cruise, and couples spa all included",2026-06-14,2026-06-21,4299.0000,EUR,1001
2002,"14-Night Grand Mediterranean Yacht Odyssey — sail Barcelona to Athens with stops in Ibiza, Amalfi, and Santorini; premium open bar, fine dining, and water sports throughout",2026-07-05,2026-07-19,6850.0000,EUR,1002
2003,"10-Night Bali Spiritual & Adventure Escape — morning yoga, volcano trekking, rice terrace cycling, nightly Kecak fire dance performances, and full board at a jungle resort",2026-08-01,2026-08-11,3199.0000,USD,1003
2004,"5-Night Dubai Ultra-Luxury City Break — penthouse at Burj Al Arab, desert falcon safari, helicopter city tour, and Michelin-starred dinners every evening",2026-09-10,2026-09-15,7499.0000,USD,1004
2005,"12-Night African Safari & Zanzibar Beach Combo — Great Migration in the Serengeti, game drives at dusk, private Zanzibar beach resort, spice farm tour, all meals included",2026-10-02,2026-10-14,5975.0000,EUR,1005
2006,"8-Night Norway Northern Lights Adventure — dog sledding, ice hotel overnight stay, snowmobile aurora tour, reindeer farm visit, and traditional Viking feast",2027-01-15,2027-01-23,5250.0000,EUR,1006
2007,"11-Night Thailand Island Hopper — Koh Samui, Koh Phi Phi, and Phuket; speedboat transfers, beach parties, Muay Thai show, and a gourmet Thai cooking class",2026-11-08,2026-11-19,2899.0000,USD,1007
2008,"9-Night Amalfi Coast & Tuscany Gourmet Tour — private villa stays, truffle hunting, wine tasting at five vineyards, pasta-making masterclass, and a private boat day",2026-05-20,2026-05-29,5100.0000,EUR,1008
2009,"6-Night Cancún All-Inclusive Sun & Surf — beachfront resort, unlimited cocktails, catamaran party boat, cenote snorkeling, and a Mayan ruins guided day trip",2026-12-01,2026-12-07,1999.0000,USD,1009
2010,"15-Night New Zealand Adventure Circuit — bungee jumping in Queenstown, Milford Sound cruise, hobbit village tour, Rotorua geothermal spa, and Tongariro Alpine Crossing",2027-03-10,2027-03-25,7200.0000,EUR,1010

 db/data/example.travel-Agencies.csv

ID,name
1001,SunEscape Holidays
1002,Wanderlust All-Inclusive
1003,Tropical Getaways Ltd.
1004,Azure Horizon Travel
1005,Paradise Found Agency
1006,Elite Escapes International
1007,Oceanic Dreams Travel
1008,Grand Voyage Co.
1009,Blissful Journeys
1010,The Luxury Travel Bureau

Run the application with

cds w

You can now navigate to http://localhost:4004/ and inspect the api endpoints and the generated test data.

Calling the agent in CAP with a query tool (read)

First, let's save the agent code into its own file srv/agent.js so that it does not clutter our service implementation. This includes the model initialization and system prompt definition. We also add a simple middleware that logs the tool calls for better transparency.

// srv/agent.js
import { OrchestrationClient } from '@sap-ai-sdk/langchain'
import { createAgent as create, createMiddleware, dynamicSystemPromptMiddleware, SystemMessage } from 'langchain'

export const model = new OrchestrationClient({
  promptTemplating: {
    model: {
      // aicore models: https://me.sap.com/notes/3437766
      // name: 'gpt-5',
      // name: 'anthropic--claude-4.5-haiku',
      name: 'mistralai--mistral-small',        // cheap for testing
    }
  }
})

const logToolCalls = createMiddleware({
  name: 'LogToolCalls',
  wrapToolCall: async (request, handler) => {
    const { toolCall: { name, args } } = request

    console.log('[tool:start]', name, args)
    try {
      const result = await handler(request)
      console.log('[tool:end]', name, result.content)
      return result
    } catch (err) {
      console.error('[tool:error]', name, err)
      throw err
    }
  },
})

const dynamicSystemPrompt = dynamicSystemPromptMiddleware(() => {
  return `Today is ${new Date().toDateString()}`
})

/** Wrapper for langchain createAgent function @type {typeof create} */
export const createAgent = (args) => {
  const systemPrompt = 'Be a helpful travel assistant.'

  return create({
    model,
    systemPrompt,
    middleware: [dynamicSystemPrompt, logToolCalls],
    ...args,
  })
}

To the fun part: integrating the agent into our CAP application. We will create a new tool that allows the agent to query available trips based on a date range, similar to the one we created in the testing section, but this time it will query our actual data model.

To query trips, we can use a CQL SELECT query in the context of TravelService.

const { Trips } = this.entities
const { SELECT } = cds.ql

// tip: to interactively test cql queries, use the cds repl
const q = SELECT.from(Trips).where `beginDate >= ${begin} and endDate <= ${end}`

Note that we explicitly use tagged template strings for the where clause. This allows us to safely inject the parameters, as cds.ql will handle the parameterization for us. This is important, as the agent might provide malicious inputs that could lead to SQL injection if not handled properly.

We can then execute this query on the TravelService, which will ensure that all the usual service logic is applied, including user permissions.

// calling TravelService => reusing user permissions
const availableTrips = await this.run(q)

Create a new file srv/travel-service.js with the following code:

// srv/travel-service.js
import cds from '@sap/cds'
import { createAgent } from './agent.js'
import { HumanMessage, tool } from 'langchain'
import { z } from 'zod'

class TravelService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {

    const tripSearch = tool(
      async ({ begin, end }) => {
        const { Trips } = this.entities
        const { SELECT } = cds.ql

        // tip: to interactively test cql queries, use the cds repl
        const q = SELECT.from(Trips).where `beginDate >= ${begin} and endDate <= ${end}`

        // calling TravelService => reusing user permissions
        const availableTrips = await this.run(q)
        return JSON.stringify(availableTrips)
      },
      {
        name: 'tripSearch',
        description: 'Find trips by date range',
        schema: z.object({
          begin: z.iso.date().describe('begin date, format YYYY-MM-DD'),
          end: z.iso.date().describe('end date, format YYYY-MM-DD')
        })
      }
    )

    const agent = createAgent({
      tools: [tripSearch]
    })

    this.on('invokeAgent', async function(req) {
      const { input } = req.data
      const userMessage = new HumanMessage(input)
      const result = await agent.invoke({ messages: [userMessage] })
      return result.messages.at(-1).content
    })

    return super.init()
  }
}

export default TravelService

This code includes the adjusted tripSearch tool and the handler for an invokeAgent action that we can use to interface with the agent.

In srv/travel-service.cds, we define the invokeAgent action, allowing us to call the agent over HTTP.

// srv/travel-service.cds
@rest
service TravelService {
    ...
    action invokeAgent(input: String) returns String;
}

Run the application with

cds w --profile hybrid

The hybrid profile lets us use the environment variables from our earlier cds bind command, so we can call the agent with the correct credentials for SAP AI Core.

To invoke the agent, create a test/http/TravelService.http file using cds add http. Since we chose the rest protocol, we can call our action at /rest/travel/invokeAgent:

# test/http/TravelService.http
@server=http://localhost:4004
@username=alice
@password=

### invokeAgent
# @NAME invokeAgent_POST
POST {{server}}/rest/travel/invokeAgent
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Basic {{username}}:{{password}}

{
  "input": "What are exciting trips for this summer? I like to go to the beach"
}

Since we added a middleware logging our tool calls, you should observe the agent calling the tripSearch tool with the appropriate date range for summer and getting the available trips as a response.

[rest] - POST /rest/travel/invokeAgent
[tool:start] tripSearch { begin: '2026-06-01', end: '2026-08-31' }
[tool:end] tripSearch [{"ID":2001,"description":"7-Night Maldives ... }]

The response contains the final answer from the agent, which should include the available trips for this summer.

You can experiment with different queries and see how the agent utilizes the tool to fetch relevant information from our CAP service.

You could also add additional parameters to the tool. For example, you could utilize the description field to search for specific interests like "beach", "skiing", or "hiking". As a simple approach, use a text search, or use vector embeddings with a cosine similarity search. With CAP, this is quite easy to implement: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/databases/vector-embeddings

Calling the agent in CAP with an action tool (write)

The query tool we implemented is useful to provide the agent with data. However, we can also implement tools that allow an agent to take action. For example, let's implement a tool that allows the agent to book a trip on behalf of the user.

In CAP, a dedicated action allows us to implement the business logic for booking a trip. Earlier, we had already defined the bound action book in our CDS model:

service TravelService {
    @readonly entity Trips as projection on travel.Trips
    actions {
        action book() returns String;
    };
    ...
}

Now we implement the corresponding event handler in srv/travel-service.js.

// srv/travel-service.js
class TravelService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    this.on('book', async function(req) {
      const { INSERT } = cds.ql
      const { Bookings } = cds.entities

      const [ trip ] = req.params
      try {
        await INSERT.into(Bookings).entries([{ trip }])
        return `trip with ID ${trip.ID} booked`
      }
      catch (e) {
        if (e.code === 'SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_PRIMARYKEY') return `Could not book trip with ID ${trip?.ID}, it has already been booked`
        else throw e;
      }
    })

    ...
  }
}

As you see, this is a very simple implementation that just inserts a new booking for the given trip. In a real application, you would usually have a multi-step process that checks for availability with the vendor, handles payment and then comes back with a confirmation. But for demonstration purposes, this implementation is sufficient to show the idea and important considerations.

The action itself is registered to the service and available to the user. However, the bookings entity is only exposed to the user as readonly.

@readonly entity Bookings as projection on travel.Bookings where $user = user;

So in the action implementation, we go directly to the database via await INSERT to insert the new booking. This database insert bypasses the service level permission checks while still maintaining the user context.

Recalling our domain model, we automatically insert the user and the booking date:

entity Bookings {
  key trip        : Association to Trips;
  key user        : User @readonly @(cds.on.insert: $user);
      bookingDate : Date default $now;
}

Now that we have implemented the action, we can expose it as a tool for our agent.

// srv/travel-service.js
class TravelService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    ...
    const bookTrip = tool(
      async ({ trip }) => {
        const result = await this.send({ event: 'book', entity: 'Trip', data: {}, params: [trip]})
        return JSON.stringify(result)
      },
      {
        name: 'bookTrip',
        description: 'Book a single trip',
        schema: z.object({
          trip: z.object({
            ID: z.string().describe('ID of the trip')
          })
        })
      }
    )
    ...
  }
}

We receive the trip id as a parameter from the model and then call the action via this.send(). This ensures that all the service level logic is applied, including permissions.

Since our agent now has the ability to book a trip, it is also useful to provide a tool for checking existing bookings, so that the agent can verify if a trip has been booked.

// srv/travel-service.js
class TravelService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    ...
    const myBookings = tool(
      async () => {
        const { Bookings } = this.entities
        const q = SELECT.from(Bookings).columns `*, trip {ID, description, beginDate, endDate}`
        const bookings = await this.run(q)
        return JSON.stringify(bookings)
      },
      {
        name: 'myBookings',
        description: 'Check the users bookings'
      }
    )
    ...
  }
}

This touches on an important aspect of agent design: providing feedback to the agent about its actions. When an agent takes an action, it should be able to observe the outcome of that action. Not only via the direct response of the action, but also by querying the state of the system.

Now that we have the necessary tools for booking a trip, let's give them to our agent and see how it utilizes them.

// srv/travel-service.js
...
    const agent = createAgent({
      tools: [tripSearch, bookTrip, myBookings]
    })
...
# test/http/TravelService.http
...
### invokeAgent
# @NAME invokeAgent_POST
POST {{server}}/rest/travel/invokeAgent
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Basic {{username}}:{{password}}

{
  "input": "Book a trip for me this summer. I like to go to the beach. Don't ask me, just book it."
}

Checking the tool calls, the agent again first searches for available trips in the summer. It then proceeds to simultaneously book two matching trips. Seems like our agent is really eager to go on vacation this summer!

[tool:start] tripSearch { begin: '2026-06-01', end: '2026-08-31' }
[tool:end] tripSearch [{"ID":2001,"description":"7-Night Maldives ... }]
[tool:start] bookTrip { trip: { ID: '2100' } }
[tool:start] bookTrip { trip: { ID: '2001' } }
[tool:end] bookTrip "trip with ID 2100 booked"
[tool:end] bookTrip "trip with ID 2001 booked"

This highlights the importance of careful agent design and implementation of guardrails. Since agents can take actions autonomously, it is crucial to ensure that they do not take unintended actions that could have negative consequences.

There are multiple dials you can use to improve the agent's behavior, such as adjusting the system prompt, providing more specific instructions in the tool descriptions. Play around with these parameters and see how it affects the agent's behavior.

But despite all instructions, there is still a chance that the agent takes unintended actions. So make sure to also implement guardrails such as confirmation steps for critical actions, or a manual review process for high-impact decisions.

What's Next

We have now seen how to implement an agentic system in CAP using the SAP AI SDK and langchain by implementing a simple travel agent that can search for trips and book them on behalf of the user.

CAP integrates nicely with the agentic workflow and allows us to easily expose our services as tools for the agent, while also ensuring that all the necessary service level logic and permissions are applied. Since production qualities like authentication, authorization, and database transactions are handled by CAP, we can focus on the domain.

As an experienced CAP developer you may have noticed... tool calls are simply another protocol for calling CAP services. This means we can also implement an adapter to expose a service as a collection of tools. But that's a story for another time.

Depending on your use case, there are a few advanced topics which may become important:

  • Input and Output: In our implementation, we have a single input and output for each session. If you want to have a conversation with multiple turns, you could keep the agent session and utilize websockets to send messages back and forth. Streaming is another tool to improve the user experience.
  • Structured output: For generating data which can then be given to a technical system, you need to ensure that the output conforms to a specific structure. Langchain already provides corresponding functionality: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/javascript/langchain/structured-output
  • Multi-agent systems: In this example, we have implemented a single agent that can perform multiple tasks. However, in more complex scenarios, you might want to implement multiple agents that specialize in different tasks and can collaborate with each other. Or you want Joule to be able to call your agent. One option is the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol. There is a blog post on wiring the agent-to-agent protocol into CAP. And as with tools, A2A is just another protocol.

But as always, start simple and grow as you go. So go ahead and implement your own agentic system in CAP, and see how it can transform your applications!

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:
The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey is now live on learning.sap and alongside it the new role-play based SAP Certified Data Architect certification is now available in this learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect

Early in my career, I sat in a room full of architects debating a major platform decision. The conversation covered integration patterns, application landscapes, and total cost of ownership. No one discussed the data. Not the quality of it, not how it would flow, not how it would be governed. We treated data as the responsibility of someone else (master data team and BW team) in a different meeting.
